metadata.title: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum / transmitted; CRIMSON = classical / blocked / exponential decay
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no sinh^2 exact formula, no four-region matching algebra, no borrowed-energy framing except debunked in one line.

beats.narration_text: A classical ball thrown at a wall it can't climb bounces back every time. A quantum particle sometimes just appears on the far side — with no energy borrowed and none repaid.

card.copy: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall

beats.narration_text: Classical rule: if your energy is less than the barrier height, you bounce. Always. Every single time. That's not a quantum thing — that's just energy conservation.
Quantum particle, same barrier, same energy — less than the barrier height. Classical prediction: total reflection. But some fraction gets through. Why?

card.copy: Classical physics predicts total reflection when E < V. Here some fraction transmits anyway. Why?

beats.narration_text: The key: the wavefunction doesn't stop at the wall. Where the barrier begins, it doesn't hit a cliff and die — it starts decaying, exponentially, into the barrier.
Exponential decay never reaches exactly zero. If the barrier is thin enough, a small but non-zero amplitude survives to the far edge — and that amplitude launches a transmitted wave.

The transmitted amplitude falls off like e to the minus two kappa L — where kappa captures how classically forbidden the region is, and L is the barrier width. Thicker barrier, exponentially less transmission.
No energy was borrowed. The particle that arrives on the far side has the same energy it had before — it tunneled through the classically forbidden region, not over it.
Tunneling is real and measurable. Alpha decay, nuclear fusion in the Sun, scanning tunneling microscopes — all run on this exponential tail surviving through classically forbidden regions.
Illustrative example: an electron with 1 eV hits a 5 eV barrier, 5 angstroms wide. The transmission is about one in ten thousand — tiny, but exactly what the exponential formula predicts.
The wavefunction leaks because exponentials never hit zero. That leak is tunneling — not magic, not borrowed energy, just what waves do inside classically forbidden regions.

card.copy: Exponentials never reach zero. The leak is the tunnel.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World series.
